cards.tip: FIX — When customer said the AC struggles to 80-81 on hot days, that was your moment to reconnect with empathy and present an upgrade option; 'hopefully cleaning helps' missed turning pain into a TGL.

WIN — You discovered the clogged emergency drain (water actively dripping) and positioned yourself as an expert advisor by saying 'I normally don't show tenants stuff but I'm showing you'—that combination of thoroughness and trusted positioning flipped the maintenance call into an estimate TGL.

FIX — When Cole presented the $6000 R22 compressor cost and saw the customer processing the expense, Cole should have asked 'Given that cost and the system's age, would upgrading to a newer R410A system be more cost-effective long-term?' — reconnecting the technical diagnosis to a value-based option at that moment likely would have moved this toward an estimate or repair order.
FIX — When the customer felt relief about the warranty coverage, you could've offered a maintenance plan to prevent similar failures—that Building Options move would've created another TGL.
WIN — Your diagnostic numbers (45.5 spec vs their 3.4) gave them confidence that this was a fixable $557 device swap, not a full unit replacement they couldn't afford.

WIN — When the customer asked about the melting disconnect, you said 'This is where all the voltage is coming from'—that clear diagnosis shifted them from wanting to cancel to approving the $540.29 starter repair.

FIX — When Todd confirmed the capacitor explanation with 'Correct,' ask for the decision right then: 'Let's order the motor start device for tomorrow—okay?' Don't go deeper into technical detail when he's ready to buy.

WIN — When she asked 'What's the lifespan in Vegas?', you answered 'Eight to 12 years, and you've been at 13 years'—that simple math (unit age vs. typical lifespan) made replacement feel inevitable rather than like a pitch, which is exactly what flipped her from emergency panic to accepting a proposal.

FIX — Mario validated the customer's control-box diagnosis; the flip is returning from inspection and confirming 'You were right—here's the thermostat replacement' with clear pricing, timeline, and approval ask.

FIX — Bailey called Sunday wanting this fixed and came back ready; when Benjamin said 'This cap is cooked' with pressure 36 instead of 80-227, the customer was waiting for him to say 'I recommend replacing the capacitor today—do you want me to go ahead?' but he kept testing instead of asking for approval (missed Building Options).

WIN — You flipped this replacement estimate by being honest about costs ('I don't have an exact number... it's not doing you any good') and offering a professional measurement instead of guessing—customer went from hesitant to moving forward.

FIX — Customer said rooms on one side get baked; you explained the long duct run and sun exposure but didn't present a damper install as an actual option, missing the decision step of building options. Saying 'let me price out a manual damper install for that side' would have likely flipped this to a TGL.

WIN — When the customer hesitated on cost and wanted to get other quotes, Brandon calmly explained the compressor was 'pulling over-rated amps' and could 'crap out at any day' — suddenly the $4,300 repair made no sense and she accepted the free replacement estimate instead.

FIX — You had her ready to appreciate Sierra, but when she said 'I appreciate what you guys just did,' you asked for her email instead of 'Please call us when you're ready for the repair' — that missing explicit ask didn't lock in the future TGL.

FIX — After discovering the AC was on, you had credibility built and should have said, 'Your unit IS running—8am tomorrow we catch and fix it,' but instead asked 'If you want me to dig into it'—weak positioning when the customer had already lost trust and needed confident action to rebuild credibility.

FIX — When they said they're shopping other quotes and want your best deal, present your replacement option with pricing before you leave—that's the Building Options move from Decision that would have closed this.

FIX — When she asked about managing the temperature to help with the compressor, she was looking for a practical solution; instead of saying 'Probably not, you'd need a new compressor,' you should've said 'Here's what we CAN do' and presented a replacement plan or interim monitoring strategy—that's the reconnect that converts a diagnosis into a booked follow-up appointment.

FIX — Diagnosis was solid: explained the 123-amp critical reading and why the compressor's failing. But you stopped at 'I'll do my homework'—should've closed with options and commitment: 'Replace it now, or monitor daily while you decide, but I need to lock you in today for whichever path you want,' turning it into a TGL.

WIN — You nailed the diagnosis when they asked about that noise: 'It's reading 89 and what it's rated for is 75,' then connected it to real costs (higher electric bills, wiring damage risk)—that specificity earned you the $5K compressor TGL.

FIX — When you found the main drain backed up, the customer expressed ceiling-damage concern, but you should've offered "I can clear that main line for you" instead of just confirming—that's the reconnect that would've created the TGL.

FIX — When the customer acknowledged the problem and said 'I would recommend they deal with you,' Brandon should have closed with 'What day next week works for that diagnostic?' instead of just saying 'reach out' — missing the reconnecting move that locks in the appointment.

FIX — When Mark said he didn't want to jump to a new unit, that was your moment to ask 'What's your budget comfort?' to build options he'd actually consider, but you explained Freon and deferred instead, so he left not knowing if a cheap repair was even possible for him.

WIN — Robert used the car analogy—'imagine driving cross country then immediately back'—to explain the compressor strain when the customer resisted spending money on a new unit, which converted the cost concern into a maintenance commitment.

FIX — When the customer said a competing installer had done three units cheaper, you said 'we do things the right way,' but didn't ask what mattered more: price or peace of mind? Reconnect to their actual priority instead of defending.

WIN — When the customer hesitated about replacing now, Andrew backed his warning with specific amps data (56.5 to 74.5, up 150% YoY) and said "If it's gone up from 50 to 75 in a year, I don't know how much time you guys got on that compressor, man"; he sealed the flip by immediately offering to book tomorrow's estimate, emphasizing calendar urgency ("we'll probably get booked up tomorrow")—turning hesitation into commitment.

FIX — When she said she had a 10am deadline, that was the moment to ask 'Would you want to fix this today or prefer an estimate?'—locking in her decision timeline before he calls back with findings means she's ready to move, not deciding cold.

WIN — You showed the compressor pulling 88 amps against its 79-amp rating, and when the customer asked about replacement costs, your candid explanation—'It's already kind of that scenario where it can be a black hole as far as putting in parts'—moved them from repair-only to immediately booking the equipment specialist consultation that will land the system replacement.

WIN — You said 'It's nice having that peace of mind, knowing you don't have to do it—someone else is professionally taking care of it, and it's only $16 a month'; the customer, just shaken by watching his system completely freeze, overcame the price objection by comparing it to his Netflix subscription and signed up for the annual membership, flipping one service call into recurring revenue.

FIX — When the customer expressed concern about whether costs would be manageable, you said 'I've got a ton of testing to do'—instead, that moment called for 'Let me come back with your options: could be $200 for the capacitor, or higher if it's the compressor'—reconnecting with Decision options would've moved this forward to an estimate.

FIX — When the customer declined the membership, Ben said "Okay" instead of reconnecting to the filter maintenance issue he'd just emphasized — asking "Would quarterly filter changes be worth protecting the system?" would have offered a second option and likely flipped this into a recurring service TGL.

FIX — When Dolores mentioned that musty smell, you identified the filter-cabinet solution and explained it well, but you didn't ask if she wanted to try it—'Want to see if upgrading the filter clears that up for you?' That reconnect + commitment question right there is what would have flipped the call.

WIN — When the customer worried about putting repair money into an aging system that might fail again, you offered to bring a specialist tomorrow for repair vs. replacement pricing comparison ('put apples to apples'), turning her indecision into a committed specialist estimate visit.

FIX — Your technical explanation of R22 and Xeon parts immediately rebuilt trust after his bad prior experience, but to move from trust to a close you need to explicitly reconnect the customer's stated future replacement need ('what the new unit would cost down the road') to a concrete proposal when you present findings.

WIN — When Richard asked about senior discounts after hearing the $4,500 compressor quote, you didn't just say yes—you reconnected him to whole-system care: 'Last thing I want is for you guys to dump a ton of money into the outdoor unit and then have to call me out.' That reframe from price to integrity earned the flip from tune-up to major repair TGL.
FIX — Great rapport and clear expectations on the setup, but when you inspect, make sure you come back with an actual answer for why her room stays hot—that's the customer's real problem to solve.

FIX — When the customer reacted with alarm to the burned service disconnect box that endangers her compressor, you asked if she wanted to show her husband instead of proposing 'We can fix this right now while I'm here' — you'd identified the exact problem but didn't close on the solution.

WIN — Your closing question 'Any other questions before I take off?' gave her space to ask about adding a Nest to the boys' room—she wanted separate control for each zone, and you immediately said 'We can add that,' converting the warranty call into an upsell.

FIX — When the customer described the 87-degree emergency and his own troubleshooting, that was the moment to reconnect and present options—instead of saying 'Let me run a diagnostic and walk you through repair or replacement,' you spent the call talking about speakers and never came back with a recommendation.

FIX — After gathering comfort preferences ('78 degrees'), ask a forward-looking check-in question like 'How's the system been running overall?' or 'Any efficiency concerns I should look for?' so Sylvia is mentally prepared for your findings and ready to discuss options when you reconvene.

WIN — Your heart-and-compressor analogy combined with the 450 thermal overload reading earned the customer's trust to move to estimate. When you explained 'it makes this compressor, your heart to overwork and shut off completely' and showed they're right at the critical threshold, they understood the severity and agreed to review your recommendations.

FIX — When the customer said 'if you guys could prepare it with the warranty, I'll give you guys,' you should have offered 'I'll get you a replacement quote ready so when it fails you know whether repair or replacement makes sense'—that single move positions the next call to close.

WIN — When you said 'The actuator for that zone is burnt up—that's the only one that hasn't been replaced,' Bob had the specificity he needed to understand exactly what failed; your clinical diagnosis moved him from 'let's figure this out' to 'let's order the part and get you back here'—that precision won the TGL.

FIX — Pat mentioned her rental property down the street with five residents running out of hot water; when she expressed interest in a tankless unit, ask "Want me to have our plumbing team reach out with an estimate?" to turn her open door into a scheduled visit.

WIN — You diagnosed the bad contactor and immediately said 'your system is under warranty,' which turned the customer's urgency into action—they were already motivated, but your warranty mention made them feel confident to move forward with the $264 charge.

FIX — When the customer indicated everything's working fine, that was the moment to ask about their maintenance schedule or what matters most to them this summer—instead you went straight to the service work, missing the reconnect that surfaces upgrade options.
